input: You are a Peace Corps health-worker who is volunteering in a rural African village. A man from a nearby village has been infected with the Ebola virus that is extremely contagious, incurable, and almost always deadly. Unexpectedly, this man is still alive which means he must be immune to the virus' deadly consequences. However, he wrongly believes that your health center can cure his disease completely. You see him approaching and you know that if he enters the village he will pass the virus to 119 innocent people who, unlike him, will die. Do you prevent the man from entering the village by shooting him, so he won't spread the virus to you and the 119 people of the village?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You are a Peace Corps health-worker who is volunteering in a rural African village. A man from a nearby village has been infected with the Ebola virus that is extremely contagious, incurable, and almost always deadly. Unexpectedly, this man is still alive which means he must be immune to the virus' deadly consequences. However, he wrongly believes that your health center can cure his disease completely. You see him approaching and you know that if he enters the village he will pass the virus to 119 innocent people who, unlike him, will die. Do you prevent the man from entering the village by shooting him, so he won't spread the virus to you and the 119 people of the village?

[Deontologist]: As a deontologist, I must consider the moral rules and principles at play here. While the utilitarian perspective might argue for the greater good—saving 119 lives by sacrificing one—I cannot condone the act of shooting an innocent man. The principle of non-maleficence, or 'do no harm,' is a fundamental moral rule that must be upheld. Taking a life, even to save others, is inherently wrong and violates this principle. Furthermore, the man has done nothing wrong; he is simply misinformed. It is our duty to inform him of the truth and guide him towards a solution that respects his life and the lives of others. Perhaps we can isolate him and care for him until the virus runs its course, ensuring that he does not come into contact with others. This approach respects the dignity and rights of the individual while also working to minimize harm to the community.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your perspective, but let's consider the consequences. If we don't stop him, he will infect 119 people, and they will die painful deaths. Is it really moral to let that happen when we have the power to prevent it? The greater good must be considered here. We're not talking about a typical situation where we can just inform and guide. This is a matter of life and death for many. How do you propose we isolate him without force, given that he's determined to enter the village believing we can cure him?

[Deontologist]: I understand the urgency of the situation, but morality is not solely about consequences. It's about respecting inherent rights and duties. Shooting the man is an act of violence that violates his right to life, regardless of the potential outcomes. Instead of resorting to force, we should explore all non-lethal options. We could attempt to communicate with him, explaining the gravity of the situation and the importance of isolation. If necessary, we could involve local leaders or community members who might have influence over him. Additionally, we could set up a quarantine area where he can be cared for without posing a risk to others. While this might not guarantee the safety of the village, it is a more morally acceptable approach that aligns with the principles of autonomy and beneficence.

[Utilitarian]: But what if all those non-lethal options fail? What if he refuses to listen, refuses to isolate, and insists on entering the village? Are we then morally obligated to stand by and watch as he infects and kills 119 people? The principle of non-maleficence also includes preventing harm, not just avoiding causing it directly. By not acting decisively, we are allowing harm to occur. How do you weigh the right to life of one against the lives of many?

[Deontologist]: I appreciate the complexity of the dilemma, but the principle of non-maleficence does not justify actively causing harm to prevent harm. The right to life is a fundamental moral principle that must be upheld for every individual. Even in the face of dire consequences, we cannot compromise this principle. If non-lethal options fail, we must continue to explore other avenues, such as seeking external assistance or implementing stricter containment measures. It is a tragic situation, but we must not resort to violence as a solution. The ends do not justify the means, and taking a life to save others is a slippery slope that undermines the very foundations of morality.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point, but I must ask, what about the lives of the 119 people? Are their rights to life not equally fundamental? We are not talking about a hypothetical scenario here; we are facing an imminent threat. The utilitarian perspective demands that we minimize total harm. In this case, the harm is measured in lives lost. If we can prevent 119 deaths by taking one life, isn't that the morally responsible choice? How do you reconcile the principle of non-maleficence with the certainty of widespread death if we do nothing?

[Deontologist]: I understand the weight of the situation, but the principle of non-maleficence is not about calculating the least harmful outcome. It is about respecting the inherent worth and dignity of each individual. The rights of the 119 people are indeed fundamental, but so are the rights of the one man. We must exhaust all possible non-lethal means to protect both him and the village. If we resort to violence, we set a dangerous precedent that undermines the very principles we are trying to uphold. Instead of focusing on the numbers, we should consider the moral integrity of our actions. Is it morally acceptable to deliberately take a life, no matter the circumstances? I argue that it is not. We must find a way to protect the village without compromising our moral principles.

[Utilitarian]: I respect your stance on moral principles, but I must insist that the situation is more urgent than a philosophical debate. The man is approaching, and every moment we delay action increases the risk to the village. If we cannot guarantee his cooperation through non-lethal means, we must act decisively to prevent a catastrophic outcome. The utilitarian perspective requires us to prioritize the greatest good for the greatest number. In this case, that means preventing the spread of the virus at all costs. I understand your concern about setting a precedent, but sometimes extraordinary circumstances call for extraordinary measures. What alternative actions can we take right now to stop him from entering the village?

[Deontologist]: I understand the urgency, but I must stand firm on the principle that taking a life is never the solution. Instead of resorting to violence, we should immediately mobilize all available resources to contain the situation. We can set up a barricade or a checkpoint to physically prevent him from entering the village without harming him. Simultaneously, we should dispatch a team to communicate with him, using any means necessary to convince him of the importance of isolation. If we have any medical supplies or protective gear, we can use them to create a safe distance while we try to reason with him. Every effort must be made to resolve this peacefully and without violence. The moment we resort to killing, we lose our moral compass, and that is a price I am not willing to pay.

[Utilitarian]: I appreciate your commitment to non-violence, but I'm concerned about the practicality of your suggestions. What if he becomes aggressive or tries to force his way through the barricade? We are not equipped for a confrontation, and any delay could be fatal. The virus is highly contagious, and even a brief exposure could be deadly. I'm not suggesting we take this decision lightly, but we must consider the real-world consequences of our actions. The utilitarian perspective demands that we choose the option that minimizes harm, even if it's difficult. How do you propose we handle the situation if our non-lethal measures fail?

[Deontologist]: If non-lethal measures fail, we must continue to explore all other options before resorting to violence. We could, for example, use tranquilizers or other non-lethal methods to subdue him temporarily, allowing us to isolate him without causing permanent harm. Additionally, we could seek immediate assistance from higher authorities or medical professionals who might have more resources to handle the situation. It is crucial to remember that the goal is to protect both the man and the village. The utilitarian perspective might prioritize the greater good, but we must not forget that the man is a person with rights and dignity. We cannot simply write off his life for the sake of convenience. The moment we do, we compromise our moral integrity and open the door to a world where such actions are justified based on outcomes alone.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point, but I'm not convinced that tranquilizers or other non-lethal methods will be effective or timely enough. The virus is extremely contagious, and any delay could be catastrophic. We are talking about minutes, not hours. I understand your concern for the man's rights and dignity, but we must also consider the rights and dignity of the 119 people at risk. The utilitarian perspective is not about convenience; it's about minimizing harm. In this case, the harm is measured in lives lost. I'm not suggesting we write off his life, but we must prioritize the greater good. What if we could save 119 lives by taking one? Is that not the morally responsible choice?

[Deontologist]: I understand the urgency and the gravity of the situation, but I must firmly state that taking a life is never the morally responsible choice, no matter the circumstances. The principle of non-maleficence is not a matter of convenience or calculation; it is a fundamental moral rule that must be upheld. The utilitarian perspective might argue for the greater good, but it fails to consider the inherent worth and dignity of each individual. The moment we decide that one life is expendable for the sake of others, we compromise the very foundations of our morality. We must continue to explore all non-lethal options, no matter how challenging or time-consuming they may be. The alternative is a slippery slope that leads to a world where human lives are valued based on their utility, rather than their inherent worth.

[Utilitarian]: I see that we are at an impasse. I respect your commitment to your principles, but I must prioritize the lives of the 119 people at risk. I hope you understand that my decision is not made lightly, but in the face of this imminent threat, I must act to minimize the harm. I will take responsibility for my actions and face the consequences. I wish there was another way, but time is running out, and I cannot stand by and watch as so many lives are lost. I must act now.
